title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 1)
text:
Program Review Two-Year Cycle (2015-2017)
Licensed Vocation Nursing (LVN) Program
Kim Parham, Assistant Director, Faculty
Hallie Coppi, Faculty
Karen Harper, Faculty
Rhonda Brown, Faculty
Cora Brownell, Director
December 2018
I. Description of the Program
The Vocational Nursing program is a three-semester program designed to prepare students to be
eligible to take the national licensing exam (NCLEX-PN) to then become active working members
in the healthcare profession. The program meets the Board of Vocation Nursing and Psychiatric
Technician (BVNPT) minimum standards of curriculum required hours: 596 theory hours and 974
clinical hours. The program also serves as the necessary prerequisite content for the graduate
looking to advance and be admitted into an LVN to RN program.
Curriculum content begins with instruction in basic areas, includes a holistic focus, and
progresses to care of individuals with more complex problems, illnesses and diseases. The
instructional plan has been developed to allow the student to build on previous knowledge and
experience in order to provide increased technical skills, incorporate understanding of health and
wellness, identify self-care and learning needs, and promote self-actualization of individuals and
families while providing care. The clinical experiences allow students to work directly with
patients in a variety of healthcare settings within the community.
a) Organization
Director of Nursing- Cora Brownell MSN, RN
Assistant Director-Kim Parham MSN, RN
Instructors approved to teach all content:
Kim Parham MSN, RN –Tenured Faculty
Hallie Coppi MSN, RN—Tenured Faculty
Rhonda Brown MSN, RN—Tenure Track Faculty
Karen Harper BSN, RN—Tenure Track Faculty
Part-Time Clinical Faculty*
Penny Huleatt, RN; Katherine Durett, RN; Eric Ricohermoso, RN;
Kara Gardner, RN
*Per BVNT requirements consist of a maximum of 15:1 ratio for instructor to
students in each clinical setting.

-- 1 of 8 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 2)
text:
b) Primary purpose
The Vocational Nursing program is based on the precept that nursing supports an
individual or family in a movement toward health and that the whole person, including the
psychological, social and physical aspects of the person's being, be considered in planning
and implementing nursing care. The educative goal of the Vocational Nursing Program is
that upon completion, the nurse will possess a broad base of nursing knowledge, an
understanding of nursing principles and the nursing process, as well as being able to
perform skills and techniques competently. Through a planned course of study, it is
expected that the students will develop a sense of ethics and integrity not only for
themselves but to the patient, the health care team and to vocational nurses as a group.
The faculty view each student as a unique individual with special abilities, talents, needs,
and goals. Cultural diversity, varying life experiences, and changing socioeconomic
factors affect each student differently. The faculty function as role model, coach,
motivator, facilitator, and resources in the learning process by providing direction for
learning and growth as a health care provider. Adult learning principles are the basis for
teaching.
c) Whom we serve include demographics
Our vocational nursing program serves a wide variety of student populations including
age (has ranged from 19-65 years of age), ethnicity, and gender (see below)*. Our program
has encompassed students across multiple counties not just those in Siskiyou County
though Siskiyou County residents do make up the largest population of entry students.
Two of our next largest counties for student population are Shasta and Jackson (Southern
Oregon).
*The presented table data includes student enrollment across ALL nursing courses and does not represent just
those of the LVN program. The data is still a reliable source; many students progress through all individual
programs (CNA, LVN, ADN).

-- 2 of 8 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 3)
text:
d) What kind of services we provide:
• BVNPT approved curriculum. The curriculum includes theory/ clinical/ skills lab/
simulation/remediation in core nursing content. Core nursing content includes
Fundamentals of Nursing, Medical-Surgical, Pharmacology, Pediatrics and
Obstetrics, and Mental Health.
• Assessment Technologies Institute (ATI), a National Council Licensure
Examination (NCLEX) vendor that provides NCLEX preparedness tutorials, quiz
banks, practice tests, and proctored tests in all areas of NCLEX content areas of
testing.
• Tutoring specific to nursing.
• First hand exposure to many areas the nursing profession has to offer once licensed.
• Partnerships with local healthcare agencies where program graduates can be
employed.
• Provide the community with qualified and competent nursing professionals.
e) How services are provided:
• Degree applicable courses
o At the end of the LVN program graduates are eligible for certificate of
Achievements and/or an Associate in Science
o
• Transferable courses for LVN to RN then to BSN.
f) Curriculum Current and needs based:
The LVN curriculum has not had a major revision since 2006 and is in dire need to be
completed revised: all courses and content. The plan is to start working on this and
have completed in 2019.
g) Class Breakdown:

-- 3 of 8 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 4)
text:
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities: Career and Technical Education (CTE), nursing
included, are expensive programs due to many of the Board regulations and requirements. This
program has been fortunate to have funding from some grants throughout the years but those
grants have not been able to fully offset the costs to run a high-quality program. As a program
we are looking at ways to increase cost savings.
b) Competition from other institutions: None. Our enrollment is typically at maximum
capacity (30 students per cohort) with waitlists for new cohort starts.
c) Requirements for four -year institutions: N/A
d) Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates:
• Having worked with the 16-week compressed calendar now for a couple of years, this
continues to pose hours requirement problems for the program. The program has a set
number of hours required by the BVNPT for any graduate to be eligible to register for
the NCLEX. The new calendar has made if more difficult to fit all of the clinical and
theory hours especially when we have overlapping cohorts. Students find the material
more rushed and not as able to digest the large amount of content we are required to
cover.
e) Non-Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates:
• Finding and retaining part-time clinical faculty has significant impact on our program
success. It is difficult to find nurses who meet both the requirements of the college and
the BVNPT. Part of the difficulty in this is the ratio of students to instructor (15:1) with
the hospital requests of having no more than 10 students per rotation group; it is also
safer to have a 10:1 ratio. With a cohort of 30 students occupying clinical time in 3
varying groups this requires minimally 4-5 faculty members to be available (taking into
account the maximum number of hours a PT faculty member can have). Many of our
PT clinical faculty view working for the college and the program as their second job
and long-term commitment can be a challenge.
• One other significant impact on the program is the availability of clinical sites and the
rural nature of our local hospitals. Though the LVN program has been very fortunate in
clinical site placement due to our strong partnerships with advisory members in our
community (many of which are previous graduates of the COS nursing program), the
lack of patient census is not always conducive to a board learning base in the clinical
setting. We have been able to augment this learning with our simulation technology
though.
f) Job market
• Requirements of prospective employers: The need for all nurses, including LVNs
is high. When looking at just Siskiyou County this may not be represented but our
graduates are being hired throughout the county and in neighboring counties.
Community employers are pleased with our graduates mainly wanting to see more
leadership experience.
• Development in field: The Department of Labor predicts a 12% increase in the
need for LVN jobs between 2016-2026. LVNs within the community are hired at
clinics, in long-term care, home health and hospice. A large majority of LVNs will

-- 4 of 8 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 5)
text:
continue in their education to the Registered Nursing (RN) level which the
Department of Labor Statistics predicts a 15% increase in need between 2016-2026.
III. Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
a) LVN Program Outcomes:
At completion of the program the student will:
1. Possess a broad base of nursing and supportive knowledge with an understanding of
nursing principles and the nursing process.
2. Assist with the implementation, and continuing evaluation of the nursing care plan.
3. Observe, record, and report to the appropriate persons symptoms, reactions, and changes
exhibited by the patient.
4. Perform selected nursing procedures with proficiency.
5. Assist with the rehabilitation of patients.
6. Demonstrate knowledge of legal and ethical considerations in nursing.
7. Apply knowledge of the basic communication process; utilize the knowledge of the
dynamics of human behavior.
8. Demonstrate knowledge of physical, psychological, and social aspects of patient care for
all age groups.
9. Assume responsibility for personal and vocational growth and development.
SLO progress:
b) Program/course/instructional improvements: The faculty independently access learning
outcomes by course and by program on a routine bases for board reports but as a college
for SLO assessment, this year (18-19) is deemed the “initialization year” in the SLO
manual, so faculty are supposed to work with the SLO committee to create program maps,
make any necessary changes to program-level outcomes, and develop their first key
assessment (to be implemented next year). Unfortunately, there is no currently-functioning
SLO committee.
c) Program’s assessment plan for next two years:
• We will continue to assess minimally half of each course’s SLO and continue to
collect data after every graduating cohort to better assess the program level
outcomes. Utilization of Canvas for course level SLO will be utilized.
IV. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
a) Our courses typically have a high completion and success rate due to it being CTE
courses. Students entering CTE typically have a clear career path and have progressed
through many other prerequisites to get to their career goal. The first semester of the
LVN program (0951) typically has the highest non-completion rate. For the two years
being reviewed (2015-2017), the follow data was given:
Course Retention Rate Average Course Retention Rate Goal
0951=95.24% 95%
0952=97.77% 100%
0953=96.59 100%
0966=100% 100%
b)

text:
Course Success Rate Course Success Rate Goal

c) FT/PT Faculty Ratio/Impact on Student Success:
a. For the complexity of the curriculum and the BVNPT requirements it is more beneficial
to have a higher number of FT faculty members. Having too many PT cover different
clinical sites or theory content sections increases student stress. Nursing students, I feel
more than any others, really need consistency for success. There are a multitude of
studies that correlate stress and lack of success in nursing school performance. This is a
high intensity program with many time demands, having consistency in who the
students can contact and rely on presentation of expectations is pivotal.
V. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative/Qualitative Research
a) Transfers, degrees, certificates, satisfaction, student contacts, Perkins data, equity data
As mentioned above, many graduates of the Vocational Nursing Program apply to the LVN to
RN Step-up program with the goal to become an RN. Graduates who meet general education
requirements can receive an Associate’s Degree in Science. Those who do not have the
general education requirements receive a certificate. See section I e) above.
COS Vocational Nursing Program received a Silver Star award from the California
Community College Chancellor’s Office for NCLEX-PN pass rates and employment figures
for 2017. Program evaluations indicate a high rate of satisfaction from program graduates and
also from employers. COS LVN graduates are sought out regionally by outreach recruiters not
only in Siskiyou County but also in Shasta County and Oregon. Annual NCLEX-PN pass rates
for the past four years are: 100%, 98%, 95%, and 100%. The statewide and national annual
averages are generally in the 75 to 85% range.
b) Summary of results of these measures
The COS Vocational Nursing Program is highly successful in student outcomes, employment
opportunities, and overall satisfaction.
c) What did you learn from the evaluation of these measures, what improvements have you
implemented or plan to implement?
The instruction is rigorous yet successful. The current curriculum needs updating to include
simulation, which can augment clinical experience and provide back-up to low hospital census
period and times with students cannot be in the hospitals due to hospital activities and reviews.
BVNPT is requiring certification of simulation instruction and simulation centers, and COS
Nursing is working on the certification process. A cost analysis of the program operations has
indicated that cost saving measures must be instituted. The program is costing more than it is
producing. COS Nursing is searching for the balance between the high demand for enrollment
by applicants and the offering of courses.
VI. Evaluation

-- 6 of 8 --

title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 7)
text:
a) Representativeness of population served: see above comments. There is not a break-down
of demographics specifically for the VN program, however in general enrollment reflects more
diversity in the VN program than in the LVN to RN program.
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery: Canvas augmented classes, EHR, Hi fidelity
simulation lab
c) Partnerships: Many of the local healthcare agencies (Fairchild Medical Center including the
Clinic, Mercy Medical Center, Public Health department, local nursery schools for pediatric
leaning objectives, Siskiyou Training and Employment Program (STEP).
d) Innovation and Implementation of best practices: Integrating learning through the use of
simulation. Many faculty members seeking higher level degrees, additional training and
advanced certifications to ensure high quality education is being delivered.
e) Efficiency in operations: Faculty are efficient in their time with students and providing
learning. More online processes have been put in place for better communication among
faculty. The utilization of Canvas has allowed a much better workflow not only for faculty but
also for students.
f) Efficiency in resource use: Having an Assistant Director in the LVN program has allowed a
better use of resources when it comes to BVNT evaluation and training requirements. It will be
tremendously helpful for completing the program’s curriculum revision.
g) Staffing: The nursing faculty currently consists of 4 full-time faculty members: 3 of which are
qualified to teach between both the LVN and the ADN programs. Having a 5 th and maybe even
a 6 th would decrease the need for so many adjuncts. Could help with retention of faculty which
would help with consistency across all courses.
h) Participation in shared governance: 3 of the 4 FT faculty members have served or are
currently serving on committees fulfilling this requirement (the 4 th is in year one which
committee duties not being recommended). All faculty regularly attend Senate and Association
meetings. These can be difficult at times due to the wide variety of hours nursing course have
but all faculty keep up to date.
i) Professional development and training: Within the last 3 years 2 of the faculty obtained their
Master’s in Nursing degrees. One faculty is pursuing a PhD. All faculty are about to complete
Simulation Certification courses and certification through a national exam. Goal of at least one
faculty is to obtain a Certified Nursing Education certificate and another has a goal of starting a
Master’s program within the next year. Nursing faculty have a licensure requirement to keep
up to date on education. There are no concerns in this area except that the college practice does
not recognize step or column movement for all of the additional training and certifications
obtained. We are hoping this will change to help support all faculty members.
j) Compliance with applicable mandates: The nursing program is in compliance with all state
regulatory agencies.
VII. Vision: To have a more progressive transition from the LVN to RN level nursing without
the gaps in our current admission process. We will have a fully certified Simulation Center
approved by both the BVNPT and the BRN. Nursing would like to have a more cohesive
and supportive connection to the rest of the college and it’s faculty.
VIII. Progress on Prior Goals:
IX. Two-year Action Plan:
• Plan is to have completely revised and updated curriculum approved and being utilized.
• Maintain our current NCLEX pass rates of 94-100% for first time attempts.
• Continue to implement cost savings to the college.

title: 2017-18 & 18-19  VN Program Review Two year cycle (Part 8)
text:
-- 7 of 8 --

• Continue to admit cohorts of 30 students with re-evaluation of admitting multiple
cohorts or only admitting after the graduation of a cohort.
• On board and retain enough faculty (both full time and part time) to effectively teach
students.
We the undersigned certify that the LVN Program, program review process included broad
dialogue about student learning, program effectiveness and resource allocation.
Kim Parham Principal Preparer
